Each record is one of: - a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or - a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn. The shell consumes the first record (the prompt with placeholders) synchronously, so the prompt shows without delay. Later records arrive as segments resolve. **Per-prompt stream.** `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` receives its context as CLI flags, streams records for one prompt cycle, and exits once every segment has resolved. A fast config produces a single record and exits at once. **Session daemon.** `oh-my-posh serve --shell=nu` starts once at shell init and lives for the session. Each prompt, the shell sends one JSON line, either to the daemon's stdin (zsh feeds it through a coprocess, PowerShell through the process object's stdin) or to a named pipe (`--request-pipe`, used by fish): ```json {"command":"render","id":42,"status":0,"execution-time":312,"pwd":"...","terminal-width":120,"env":{"...":"..."}} ``` Records come back on stdout in the same format as `stream`, except each payload is prefixed with the request id and a `\x1f` separator (`42\x1f\0`) so the shell can discard records from a superseded cycle. Two more commands complete the protocol: `abort` cancels the in-flight render cycle (sent on Enter), and `quit` shuts the daemon down (sent on shell exit; losing its stdout pipe also kills it, so a crashed shell never leaks a daemon). The generated init scripts prefer the daemon, fall back to a per-prompt `stream` process when it fails (after three failures the script abandons the daemon for the session), and fall back to classic synchronous rendering when both are unavailable. ## How the four supported shells do it Each shell uses whatever async primitive, daemon transport, and repaint API it has: | Shell | Async notification | Daemon requests via | Repaint call | | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | coprocess stdin | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | named pipe (`--request-pipe`) | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | process object stdin | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | daemon stdin | `clink.refilterprompt()` | In every case the repaint API redraws the prompt while keeping the user's partially typed command line intact. That API is the load-bearing piece. ### Sequence flow: per-prompt stream (zsh as the reference) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant O as oh-my-posh stream S->>O: spawn with context (status, duration, pwd, ...) O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders Note over S: blocking read, ~instant S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt S->>S: cache transient prompt Note over O: all segments resolved O-->>S: EOF, process exits U->>S: presses Enter S->>S: close fd (stops any in-flight stream),
print cached transient prompt S->>U: command executes ``` ### Sequence flow: session daemon The daemon path is the same cycle without the spawn, repeated for every prompt: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant D as oh-my-posh serve Note over S,D: daemon started once at shell init Note over S: previous command finished,
new prompt due S->>D: {"command":"render","id":42,...}
(stdin or named pipe) D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 S->>S: id matches current cycle: repaint in place D-->>S: 42\x1f\x1e\0 S->>S: cache transient prompt U->>S: presses Enter S->>D: {"command":"abort"} S->>U: cached transient prompt, command executes Note over S,D: daemon stays alive for the next prompt ``` ## Where this touches the current Nushell integration Today's init script (generated by `oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous. The relevant part: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| # gather context (cleared screen detection, SET_POSHCONTEXT) ... _omp_get_prompt primary $"--cleared=($clear)" } $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } ``` `_omp_get_prompt` calls `^oh-my-posh print primary ...` and blocks until every segment has finished. A slow git repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is where the streaming path slots in: send the daemon a render request (or spawn `oh-my-posh stream` as the fallback), return the first record, and let later records repaint. The daemon itself would start once, during init, next to where the script renders `$env.PROMPT_MULTILINE_INDICATOR`. The rest of the feature maps onto existing Nushell hooks: - **Transient prompt** works today via `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`; the streamed transient record would only make it cheaper (serve it from cache instead of spawning a process on every Enter). - **Stop on accept** (requirement 4) maps onto the existing `pre_execution` hook: send the daemon an `abort`, or kill the fallback stream process. - **Daemon shutdown** needs no hook at all: the daemon exits when its stdout pipe closes, so it dies with the shell even when the shell never gets to say `quit`. ## What Nushell is missing Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle, converts the result to a string, and hands it to reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From that point until the line is submitted, two things are impossible: 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the stream, and `job send 0` can queue a message for the main thread, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only processes key events. There is no fd watcher, no signal handler, no timer, and no job-message delivery that fires while the editor waits for input. (`SIGUSR1`, which the fish integration relies on, would not fit Nushell's cross-platform model anyway; a channel-based wake fits better.) 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, and Nushell already exposes it in `$env.config.keybindings` as `event: { send: repaint }`, but only a keypress can trigger it, and when it fires, reedline re-queries the `Prompt` trait and Nushell hands back the same stored string. Binding a key to `repaint` and pressing it mid-edit demonstrates both halves of the gap: the redraw itself is safe and buffer-preserving, yet the content never changes. There is no way to say "the prompt is now this new string, redraw it". Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing in another feature: the `ExternalPrinter` is a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle with one difference: the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. ## The ask A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work for us, in order of preference: ### Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred) Expose a way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a `commandline` subcommand: ```nu commandline set-prompt --left $new_prompt # swap content + repaint, buffer untouched ``` Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a bounded channel created by the host, polled in reedline's `read_line()` event loop; on receipt, update the prompt content held by the host's `Prompt` object and repaint. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation, and this stays independent of `PROMPT_COMMAND` semantics. ### Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation A way for a background job to request a full prompt refresh, where Nushell re-runs `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` (and `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT`) and repaints in place. This also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and has trickier semantics (the closure runs on the engine while a command line is being edited). ### What the resulting integration would look like With Option A, the streaming path in `omp.nu` would be roughly: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| # spawn the stream; read records in the background job spawn { ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records | each {|record| if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { # \x1e prefix: cache the transient prompt for later $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) } else { commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability } } } # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly } ``` (Exact plumbing aside: everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today.) The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session instead of one `stream` process per prompt, `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a JSON render request to it, and the reader discards records from stale cycles. The named-pipe transport exists precisely because fish cannot write to a running process's stdin either; Nushell could reuse that flag. The missing capability is identical. ### Sequence flow of the proposed integration ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant R as reedline (read_line) participant N as Nushell engine participant J as background job participant O as oh-my-posh stream N->>N: PROMPT_COMMAND runs N->>O: spawn stream O-->>N: record 1 (placeholders) N->>J: job spawn (reader) N->>R: read_line(prompt = record 1) R->>U: display prompt U->>R: starts typing O-->>J: record 2 (segment resolved) J->>R: prompt-update channel: record 2 Note over R: poll wakes, swap prompt, repaint,
buffer and cursor preserved R->>U: updated prompt, input intact U->>R: presses Enter R->>N: Signal::Success N->>J: pre_execution hook: job kill (stops stream) ``` ## Guarantees from our side To keep the shell-side risk low, the Oh My Posh contract is strict: - Every record is a complete prompt string, already escaped for the target shell. The shell never assembles or diffs anything. - Records are strictly newer-wins; applying only the latest received record is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates. In daemon mode the id prefix also lets the shell drop records from a superseded cycle. - The `stream` process exits on its own once all segments resolve; both it and the daemon die on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away, so there is nothing to reap in the happy path. - The integration degrades gracefully: daemon failure falls back to a per-prompt `stream` process, and if that fails too, to today's synchronous rendering. Users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

-Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders
-immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call, a language version lookup) show a placeholder,
-and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink).
-We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active.
-This document explains how the feature works, where it touches the current Nushell integration, and what we need from
-Nushell (and reedline).

+Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call, a language version lookup) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. This document explains how the feature works, where it touches the current Nushell integration, and what we need from Nushell (and reedline).
## What streaming does
With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows:
1. All segments execute concurrently, each with a 100ms budget.
-2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder`
-   (default `...`) and keep executing in the background.
+2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder` (default `...`) and keep executing in the background.
3. Oh My Posh emits the full prompt, placeholders included, immediately. The user can start typing right away.
4. Each time a pending segment completes, Oh My Posh re-renders the full prompt and emits it again.
5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed.
-The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every
-prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment
-caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime.

+The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime.
## The division of labor
Oh My Posh does all the heavy lifting. The shell integration is deliberately thin.
-**Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial
-updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved).

+**Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved).
**The shell must provide four things:**
-1. **Spawn and read:** start the renderer (the daemon at init, or a `stream` process per prompt) in the background and
-   read its stdout without blocking the REPL.

2. **Request per prompt (daemon mode):** send the daemon a render request with fresh context when a new prompt is due.
-3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with
-   the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position.
-4. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint
-   over command output (kill the `stream` process, or send the daemon an `abort`).
+3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position.

attachment.snippet:
1 # Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell 2 3 Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call, a language version lookup) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. This document explains how the feature works, where it touches the current Nushell integration, and what we need from Nushell (and reedline). 4 5 ## What streaming does 6 7 Users enable it with a single config property, the per-segment time budget in milliseconds: 8 9 ```json 10 { 11 "streaming": 100 12 } 13 ``` 14 15 With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows: 16 17 1. All segments execute concurrently, each with a 100ms budget. 18 2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder` (default `...`) and keep executing in the background. 19 3. Oh My Posh emits the full prompt, placeholders included, immediately. The user can start typing right away. 20 4. Each time a pending segment completes, Oh My Posh re-renders the full prompt and emits it again. 21 5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed. 22 23 The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime. 24 25 ## The division of labor 26 27 Oh My Posh does all the heavy lifting. The shell integration is deliberately thin. 28 29 **Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved). 30 31 **The shell must provide four things:** 32 33 1. **Spawn and read:** start the renderer (the daemon at init, or a `stream` process per prompt) in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 34 2. **Request per prompt (daemon mode):** send the daemon a render request with fresh context when a new prompt is due. 35 3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position. 36 4. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output (kill the `stream` process, or send the daemon an `abort`). 37 38 Requirement 3 is the one Nushell lacks. 39 40 ### The wire protocol 41 42 Both mechanisms write null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to stdout. Each record is one of: 43 44 - a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or 45 - a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn. 46 47 The shell consumes the first record (the prompt with placeholders) synchronously, so the prompt shows without delay. Later records arrive as segments resolve. 48 49 **Per-prompt stream.** `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` receives its context as CLI flags, streams records for one prompt cycle, and exits once every segment has resolved. A fast config produces a single record and exits at once. 50 51 **Session daemon.** `oh-my-posh serve --shell=nu` starts once at shell init and lives for the session. Each prompt, the shell sends one JSON line, either to the daemon's stdin (zsh feeds it through a coprocess, PowerShell through the process object's stdin) or to a named pipe (`--request-pipe`, used by fish): 52 53 ```json 54 {"command":"render","id":42,"status":0,"execution-time":312,"pwd":"...","terminal-width":120,"env":{"...":"..."}} 55 ``` 56 57 Records come back on stdout in the same format as `stream`, except each payload is prefixed with the request id and a `\x1f` separator (`42\x1f\0`) so the shell can discard records from a superseded cycle. Two more commands complete the protocol: `abort` cancels the in-flight render cycle (sent on Enter), and `quit` shuts the daemon down (sent on shell exit; losing its stdout pipe also kills it, so a crashed shell never leaks a daemon). 58 59 The generated init scripts prefer the daemon, fall back to a per-prompt `stream` process when it fails (after three failures the script abandons the daemon for the session), and fall back to classic synchronous rendering when both are unavailable. 60 61 ## How the four supported shells do it 62 63 Each shell uses whatever async primitive, daemon transport, and repaint API it has: 64 65 | Shell | Async notification | Daemon requests via | Repaint call | 66 | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | 67 | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | coprocess stdin | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | 68 | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | named pipe (`--request-pipe`) | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | 69 | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | process object stdin | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | 70 | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | daemon stdin | `clink.refilterprompt()` | 71 72 In every case the repaint API redraws the prompt while keeping the user's partially typed command line intact. That API is the load-bearing piece. 73 74 ### Sequence flow: per-prompt stream (zsh as the reference) 75 76 ```mermaid 77 sequenceDiagram 78 participant U as User 79 participant S as Shell (line editor) 80 participant O as oh-my-posh stream 81 82 S->>O: spawn with context (status, duration, pwd, ...) 83 O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders 84 Note over S: blocking read, ~instant 85 S->>U: display prompt 86 U->>S: starts typing 87 Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms 88 O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in 89 Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) 90 S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt 91 Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched 92 O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt 93 S->>S: cache transient prompt 94 Note over O: all segments resolved 95 O-->>S: EOF, process exits 96 U->>S: presses Enter 97 S->>S: close fd (stops any in-flight stream),
print cached transient prompt 98 S->>U: command executes 99 ``` 100 101 ### Sequence flow: session daemon 102 103 The daemon path is the same cycle without the spawn, repeated for every prompt: 104 105 ```mermaid 106 sequenceDiagram 107 participant U as User 108 participant S as Shell (line editor) 109 participant D as oh-my-posh serve 110 111 Note over S,D: daemon started once at shell init 112 Note over S: previous command finished,
new prompt due 113 S->>D: {"command":"render","id":42,...}
(stdin or named pipe) 114 D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 115 S->>U: display prompt 116 U->>S: starts typing 117 D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 118 S->>S: id matches current cycle: repaint in place 119 D-->>S: 42\x1f\x1e\0 120 S->>S: cache transient prompt 121 U->>S: presses Enter ... [117 lines truncated] ...

content.content:
121 U->>S: presses Enter 122 S->>D: {"command":"abort"} 123 S->>U: cached transient prompt, command executes 124 Note over S,D: daemon stays alive for the next prompt 125 ``` 126 127 ## Where this touches the current Nushell integration 128 129 Today's init script (generated by `oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous. The relevant part: 130 131 ```nu 132 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| 133 # gather context (cleared screen detection, SET_POSHCONTEXT) ... 134 _omp_get_prompt primary $"--cleared=($clear)" 135 } 136 137 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } 138 ``` 139 140 `_omp_get_prompt` calls `^oh-my-posh print primary ...` and blocks until every segment has finished. A slow git repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is where the streaming path slots in: send the daemon a render request (or spawn `oh-my-posh stream` as the fallback), return the first record, and let later records repaint. The daemon itself would start once, during init, next to where the script renders `$env.PROMPT_MULTILINE_INDICATOR`. 141 142 The rest of the feature maps onto existing Nushell hooks: 143 144 - **Transient prompt** works today via `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`; the streamed transient record would only make it cheaper (serve it from cache instead of spawning a process on every Enter). 145 - **Stop on accept** (requirement 4) maps onto the existing `pre_execution` hook: send the daemon an `abort`, or kill the fallback stream process. 146 - **Daemon shutdown** needs no hook at all: the daemon exits when its stdout pipe closes, so it dies with the shell even when the shell never gets to say `quit`. 147 148 ## What Nushell is missing 149 150 Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle, converts the result to a string, and hands it to reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From that point until the line is submitted, two things are impossible: 151 152 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the stream, and `job send 0` can queue a message for the main thread, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only processes key events. There is no fd watcher, no signal handler, no timer, and no job-message delivery that fires while the editor waits for input. (`SIGUSR1`, which the fish integration relies on, would not fit Nushell's cross-platform model anyway; a channel-based wake fits better.) 153 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, and Nushell already exposes it in `$env.config.keybindings` as `event: { send: repaint }`, but only a keypress can trigger it, and when it fires, reedline re-queries the `Prompt` trait and Nushell hands back the same stored string. Binding a key to `repaint` and pressing it mid-edit demonstrates both halves of the gap: the redraw itself is safe and buffer-preserving, yet the content never changes. There is no way to say "the prompt is now this new string, redraw it". 154 155 Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing in another feature: the `ExternalPrinter` is a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle with one difference: the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. 156 157 ## The ask 158 159 A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work for us, in order of preference: 160 161 ### Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred) 162 163 Expose a way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a `commandline` subcommand: 164 165 ```nu 166 commandline set-prompt --left $new_prompt # swap content + repaint, buffer untouched 167 ``` 168 169 Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a bounded channel created by the host, polled in reedline's `read_line()` event loop; on receipt, update the prompt content held by the host's `Prompt` object and repaint. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation, and this stays independent of `PROMPT_COMMAND` semantics. 170 171 ### Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation 172 173 A way for a background job to request a full prompt refresh, where Nushell re-runs `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` (and `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT`) and repaints in place. This also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and has trickier semantics (the closure runs on the engine while a command line is being edited). 174 175 ### What the resulting integration would look like 176 177 With Option A, the streaming path in `omp.nu` would be roughly: 178 179 ```nu 180 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| 181 # spawn the stream; read records in the background 182 job spawn { 183 ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... 184 | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records 185 | each {|record| 186 if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { 187 # \x1e prefix: cache the transient prompt for later 188 $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) 189 } else { 190 commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability 191 } 192 } 193 } 194 195 # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly 196 } 197 ``` 198 199 (Exact plumbing aside: everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today.) 200 201 The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session instead of one `stream` process per prompt, `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a JSON render request to it, and the reader discards records from stale cycles. The named-pipe transport exists precisely because fish cannot write to a running process's stdin either; Nushell could reuse that flag. The missing capability is identical. 202 203 ### Sequence flow of the proposed integration 204 205 ```mermaid 206 sequenceDiagram 207 participant U as User 208 participant R as reedline (read_line) 209 participant N as Nushell engine 210 participant J as background job 211 participant O as oh-my-posh stream 212 213 N->>N: PROMPT_COMMAND runs 214 N->>O: spawn stream 215 O-->>N: record 1 (placeholders) 216 N->>J: job spawn (reader) 217 N->>R: read_line(prompt = record 1) 218 R->>U: display prompt 219 U->>R: starts typing 220 O-->>J: record 2 (segment resolved) 221 J->>R: prompt-update channel: record 2 222 Note over R: poll wakes, swap prompt, repaint,
buffer and cursor preserved 223 R->>U: updated prompt, input intact 224 U->>R: presses Enter 225 R->>N: Signal::Success 226 N->>J: pre_execution hook: job kill (stops stream) 227 ``` 228 229 ## Guarantees from our side 230 231 To keep the shell-side risk low, the Oh My Posh contract is strict: 232 233 - Every record is a complete prompt string, already escaped for the target shell. The shell never assembles or diffs anything. 234 - Records are strictly newer-wins; applying only the latest received record is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates. In daemon mode the id prefix also lets the shell drop records from a superseded cycle. 235 - The `stream` process exits on its own once all segments resolve; both it and the daemon die on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away, so there is nothing to reap in the happy path. 236 - The integration degrades gracefully: daemon failure falls back to a per-prompt `stream` process, and if that fails too, to today's synchronous rendering. Users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. 237 238 We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.** 239

# Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. ## How it works Users set `"streaming": 100` in their config: a per-segment budget in milliseconds. Segments that exceed it render a placeholder and keep executing; each completion re-renders the full prompt. The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session, which renders every prompt on request (no process spawn per prompt) with a per-prompt `oh-my-posh stream` process as fallback. Both emit the same protocol: null-byte (`\0`) delimited records on stdout, each a complete, fully escaped prompt string, ready to display as-is. A `\x1e` prefix marks a transient-prompt record the shell caches for Enter. The daemon takes one JSON request per prompt (`{"command":"render","id":42,...}`, via stdin or a named pipe) and prefixes its records with the request id so stale ones can be dropped; `abort` cancels an in-flight cycle, and losing its stdout pipe kills it, so it dies with the shell. The shell-side integration is thin. It must: 1. **Spawn and read:** run the renderer in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 2. **Repaint on data:** when a record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. 3. **Stop on accept:** on Enter or Ctrl-C, stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output. Requirement 2 is the one Nushell lacks. The four supported shells each solve it with a native async primitive plus a buffer-preserving repaint call: | Shell | Async notification | Repaint call | | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | `clink.refilterprompt()` | The cycle, with zsh as the reference: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant O as oh-my-posh S->>O: render request (status, duration, pwd, ...) O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt S->>S: cache transient prompt U->>S: presses Enter S->>S: stop updates,
print cached transient prompt S->>U: command executes ``` ## Where this touches the Nushell integration Today's init script (`oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| _omp_get_prompt primary } $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } ``` `_omp_get_prompt` blocks until every segment has finished; a slow git repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is where streaming slots in: request a render, return the first record, and let later records repaint. Everything else maps onto what Nushell already has: the daemon starts once at init and dies with its stdout pipe, the transient prompt uses `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`, and the `pre_execution` hook covers stop-on-accept. ## What Nushell is missing Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle and hands the result to reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From then until the line is submitted, two things are impossible: 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the records, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only processes key events. There is no fd watcher, timer, or job-message delivery that fires while the editor waits for input. 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, and Nushell exposes it as `event: { send: repaint }` in `$env.config.keybindings`, but only a keypress can trigger it, and when it fires, Nushell hands reedline the same stored string. Bind a key to `repaint` and press it mid-edit: the redraw is safe and buffer-preserving, yet the content never changes. Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing: the `ExternalPrinter` is a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle, except the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. ## The ask A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work, in order of preference: **Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred).** A way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a `commandline set-prompt` subcommand. Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a channel polled in reedline's `read_line()` loop that updates the prompt content and repaints. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation. **Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation.** A way for a background job to make Nushell re-run `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` and repaint in place. Also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and runs user code on the engine mid-edit. With Option A, the integration would be roughly: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| job spawn { ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records | each {|record| if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) } else { commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability } } } # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly } ``` Everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today. The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session, and `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a render request per prompt. ## Guarantees from our side - Every record is a complete prompt string; applying only the latest one is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates and the shell never assembles or diffs anything. - The renderer dies on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away; nothing to reap. - On any failure the integration falls back to today's synchronous rendering, and users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

lines: # Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell

-Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call, a language version lookup) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. This document explains how the feature works, where it touches the current Nushell integration, and what we need from Nushell (and reedline).

+Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active.
-## What streaming does
+## How it works
-Users enable it with a single config property, the per-segment time budget in milliseconds:

+Users set `"streaming": 100` in their config: a per-segment budget in milliseconds. Segments that exceed it render a placeholder and keep executing; each completion re-renders the full prompt. The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session, which renders every prompt on request (no process spawn per prompt) with a per-prompt `oh-my-posh stream` process as fallback.
-```json
-  "streaming": 100

+Both emit the same protocol: null-byte (`\0`) delimited records on stdout, each a complete, fully escaped prompt string, ready to display as-is. A `\x1e` prefix marks a transient-prompt record the shell caches for Enter. The daemon takes one JSON request per prompt (`{"command":"render","id":42,...}`, via stdin or a named pipe) and prefixes its records with the request id so stale ones can be dropped; `abort` cancels an in-flight cycle, and losing its stdout pipe kills it, so it dies with the shell.
-With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows:
+The shell-side integration is thin. It must:
-1. All segments execute concurrently, each with a 100ms budget.
-2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder` (default `...`) and keep executing in the background.
-3. Oh My Posh emits the full prompt, placeholders included, immediately. The user can start typing right away.
-4. Each time a pending segment completes, Oh My Posh re-renders the full prompt and emits it again.
-5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed.
+1. **Spawn and read:** run the renderer in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL.
+2. **Repaint on data:** when a record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor.
+3. **Stop on accept:** on Enter or Ctrl-C, stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output.

-The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime.
+Requirement 2 is the one Nushell lacks. The four supported shells each solve it with a native async primitive plus a buffer-preserving repaint call:
-## The division of labor
+| Shell      | Async notification                                         | Repaint call                                               |
+| zsh        | `zle -F <fd> handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor)   | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt`                         |
+| fish       | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint`         |
+| PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue     | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` |
+| cmd        | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon                       | `clink.refilterprompt()`                                   |
-Oh My Posh does all the heavy lifting. The shell integration is deliberately thin.
+The cycle, with zsh as the reference:

-**Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved).
-**The shell must provide four things:**
-1. **Spawn and read:** start the renderer (the daemon at init, or a `stream` process per prompt) in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL.
-2. **Request per prompt (daemon mode):** send the daemon a render request with fresh context when a new prompt is due.
-3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position.
-4. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output (kill the `stream` process, or send the daemon an `abort`).
-Requirement 3 is the one Nushell lacks.
-### The wire protocol
-Both mechanisms write null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to stdout. Each record is one of:
-- a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or
-- a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn.
-The shell consumes the first record (the prompt with placeholders) synchronously, so the prompt shows without delay. Later records arrive as segments resolve.

-**Per-prompt stream.** `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` receives its context as CLI flags, streams records for one prompt cycle, and exits once every segment has resolved. A fast config produces a single record and exits at once.

-**Session daemon.** `oh-my-posh serve --shell=nu` starts once at shell init and lives for the session. Each prompt, the shell sends one JSON line, either to the daemon's stdin (zsh feeds it through a coprocess, PowerShell through the process object's stdin) or to a named pipe (`--request-pipe`, used by fish):
-```json
-{"command":"render","id":42,"status":0,"execution-time":312,"pwd":"...","terminal-width":120,"env":{"...":"..."}}

-Records come back on stdout in the same format as `stream`, except each payload is prefixed with the request id and a `\x1f` separator (`42\x1f<prompt>\0`) so the shell can discard records from a superseded cycle. Two more commands complete the protocol: `abort` cancels the in-flight render cycle (sent on Enter), and `quit` shuts the daemon down (sent on shell exit; losing its stdout pipe also kills it, so a crashed shell never leaks a daemon).

-The generated init scripts prefer the daemon, fall back to a per-prompt `stream` process when it fails (after three failures the script abandons the daemon for the session), and fall back to classic synchronous rendering when both are unavailable.
-## How the four supported shells do it
-Each shell uses whatever async primitive, daemon transport, and repaint API it has:
-| Shell      | Async notification                                         | Daemon requests via              | Repaint call                                               |
-| zsh        | `zle -F <fd> handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor)   | coprocess stdin                  | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt`                         |
-| fish       | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | named pipe (`--request-pipe`)    | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint`         |
-| PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue     | process object stdin             | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` |
-| cmd        | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon                       | daemon stdin                     | `clink.refilterprompt()`                                   |
-In every case the repaint API redraws the prompt while keeping the user's partially typed command line intact. That API is the load-bearing piece.
-### Sequence flow: per-prompt stream (zsh as the reference)
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
participant U as User
participant S as Shell (line editor)
-    participant O as oh-my-posh stream
+    participant O as oh-my-posh
-    S->>O: spawn with context (status, duration, pwd, ...)
+    S->>O: render request (status, duration, pwd, ...)
O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders
-    Note over S: blocking read, ~instant
S->>U: display prompt
U->>S: starts typing
Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms

Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched
O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt
S->>S: cache transient prompt
-    Note over O: all segments resolved
-    O-->>S: EOF, process exits
U->>S: presses Enter

- S->>S: close fd (stops any in-flight stream),
print cached transient prompt

+ S->>S: stop updates,
print cached transient prompt
S->>U: command executes
-### Sequence flow: session daemon
+## Where this touches the Nushell integration
-The daemon path is the same cycle without the spawn, repeated for every prompt:
+Today's init script (`oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous:
-```mermaid
-sequenceDiagram
-    participant U as User
-    participant S as Shell (line editor)
-    participant D as oh-my-posh serve
-    Note over S,D: daemon started once at shell init

- Note over S: previous command finished,
new prompt due

- S->>D: {"command":"render","id":42,...}
(stdin or named pipe)
-    D-->>S: 42\x1f<prompt with placeholders>\0
-    S->>U: display prompt
-    U->>S: starts typing
-    D-->>S: 42\x1f<prompt, git segment filled in>\0
-    S->>S: id matches current cycle: repaint in place
-    D-->>S: 42\x1f\x1e<transient prompt>\0
-    S->>S: cache transient prompt
-    U->>S: presses Enter
-    S->>D: {"command":"abort"}
-    S->>U: cached transient prompt, command executes
-    Note over S,D: daemon stays alive for the next prompt
-## Where this touches the current Nushell integration
-Today's init script (generated by `oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous. The relevant part:
```nu

-    _omp_get_prompt primary $"--cleared=($clear)"

$env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right }

-- **Daemon shutdown** needs no hook at all: the daemon exits when its stdout pipe closes, so it dies with the shell even when the shell never gets to say `quit`.

-A way for a background job to request a full prompt refresh, where Nushell re-runs `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` (and `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT`) and repaints in place. This also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and has trickier semantics (the closure runs on the engine while a command line is being edited).
-### What the resulting integration would look like
-With Option A, the streaming path in `omp.nu` would be roughly:
```nu
$env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {||
-    # spawn the stream; read records in the background
job spawn {
^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context...
| split row (char --integer 0)                   # \0 delimited records
| each {|record|
if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) {
-                # \x1e prefix: cache the transient prompt for later
$env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..)
} else {
commandline set-prompt --left $record    # <- the missing capability
# ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly
-(Exact plumbing aside: everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today.)

+Everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today. The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session, and `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a render request per prompt.

-The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session instead of one `stream` process per prompt, `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a JSON render request to it, and the reader discards records from stale cycles. The named-pipe transport exists precisely because fish cannot write to a running process's stdin either; Nushell could reuse that flag. The missing capability is identical.
-### Sequence flow of the proposed integration
-```mermaid
-sequenceDiagram
-    participant U as User
-    participant R as reedline (read_line)
-    participant N as Nushell engine
-    participant J as background job
-    participant O as oh-my-posh stream
-    N->>N: PROMPT_COMMAND runs
-    N->>O: spawn stream
-    O-->>N: record 1 (placeholders)
-    N->>J: job spawn (reader)
-    N->>R: read_line(prompt = record 1)
-    R->>U: display prompt
-    U->>R: starts typing
-    O-->>J: record 2 (segment resolved)
-    J->>R: prompt-update channel: record 2

- Note over R: poll wakes, swap prompt, repaint,
buffer and cursor preserved
-    R->>U: updated prompt, input intact
-    U->>R: presses Enter
-    R->>N: Signal::Success
-    N->>J: pre_execution hook: job kill (stops stream)
## Guarantees from our side
-To keep the shell-side risk low, the Oh My Posh contract is strict:
+- Every record is a complete prompt string; applying only the latest one is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates and the shell never assembles or diffs anything.
+- The renderer dies on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away; nothing to reap.
+- On any failure the integration falls back to today's synchronous rendering, and users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior.
-- Every record is a complete prompt string, already escaped for the target shell. The shell never assembles or diffs anything.

-- Records are strictly newer-wins; applying only the latest received record is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates. In daemon mode the id prefix also lets the shell drop records from a superseded cycle.
-- The `stream` process exits on its own once all segments resolve; both it and the daemon die on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away, so there is nothing to reap in the happy path.

-- The integration degrades gracefully: daemon failure falls back to a per-prompt `stream` process, and if that fails too, to today's synchronous rendering. Users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior.

We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

content.thinking:
I'm reviewing the document with the writing rules in mind—focusing on active voice, positive framing, concrete language, and eliminating unnecessary words. Looking at the opening paragraph about Oh My Posh's streaming feature, I need to tighten how it explains the immediate rendering and handling of slow operations like git status checks.

For the "How it works" section, I'm examining the explanation of the streaming configuration and the daemon setup. The description of how segments with millisecond budgets render placeholders while continuing to execute is solid, though I could make it more direct. The part about the daemon rendering prompts on request versus spawning processes per prompt needs careful review to ensure it's as clear and concise as possible. The daemon accepts JSON requests per prompt with an id field, prefixes its output records with that id so the shell can discard stale responses, and supports an `abort` command to cancel in-flight cycles; it terminates when its stdout pipe closes. The shell-side integration is minimal—it just needs to handle the async primitives and buffer-preserving repaints, which the four supported shells each implement differently using their native async mechanisms.

For Nushell specifically, the current init script is fully synchronous and blocks the entire prompt on slow operations like git checks, but streaming can be integrated by requesting a render, returning the first record immediately, and letting subsequent records trigger repaints. The rest maps cleanly onto Nushell's existing features: the daemon lifecycle, the transient prompt command, and the pre-execution hook for stop-on-accept behavior.

However, Nushell has two fundamental limitations that prevent true streaming. First, nothing external can wake the REPL while it's waiting for input—there's no file descriptor watcher, timer, or job-message delivery mechanism that fires during editor input, only key events can interrupt the parked main thread. Second, even though Nushell exposes a repaint event through keybindings, it only triggers on keypresses and always redraws the same stored prompt string, so mid-edit content updates aren't possible.

Reedline already has the infrastructure needed: an `ExternalPrinter` channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during input and uses to safely redraw above the prompt while preserving the buffer. Asynchronous prompt updates would need the same wake-and-redraw cycle, but with the payload replacing the prompt itself instead of printing above it.

For the actual mechanism, a background job could hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint—something like a `commandline set-prompt` subcommand that internally mirrors the `ExternalPrinter` design: a channel polled in reedline's loop that updates the prompt and repaints. Since Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, there's no need for re-evaluation.

Option B would also work—having a background job trigger `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` re-evaluation and repaint in place—but that costs a closure evaluation per update and runs user code on the engine mid-edit. With Option A, the integration is straightforward, and everything except the `commandline set-prompt` subcommand is already expressible in Nushell. The daemon variant follows the same pattern: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session, and `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a render request per prompt.

The guarantees are solid: each record is a complete prompt string, so applying only the latest one is always correct and allows slow consumers to coalesce updates without the shell needing to assemble or diff anything. The renderer dies cleanly on SIGPIPE or EOF when the shell exits, so there's nothing to reap. If anything fails, the integration falls back to synchronous rendering, and users without `streaming` in their config see no change at all.

The plan is to build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration like we do for other shells, adjusting the protocol if needed. The key capability that unblocks this—and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem like starship—is letting a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.

I should also swap "We would like to support" for "We want to support" and apply these edits through the system, then run Vale to check.

# Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. ## How it works Users set `"streaming": 100` in their config: a per-segment budget in milliseconds. Segments that exceed it render a placeholder and keep executing; each completion re-renders the full prompt. The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session, which renders every prompt on request (no process spawn per prompt) with a per-prompt `oh-my-posh stream` process as fallback. Both emit the same protocol: null-byte (`\0`) delimited records on stdout, each a complete, fully escaped prompt string, ready to display as-is. A `\x1e` prefix marks a transient-prompt record the shell caches for Enter. The daemon takes one JSON request per prompt (`{"command":"render","id":42,...}`, via stdin or a named pipe) and prefixes its records with the request id so stale ones can be dropped; `abort` cancels an in-flight cycle, and losing its stdout pipe kills it, so it dies with the shell. The shell-side integration is thin. It must: 1. **Spawn and read:** run the renderer in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 2. **Repaint on data:** when a record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. 3. **Stop on accept:** on Enter or Ctrl-C, stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output. Requirement 2 is the one Nushell lacks. The four supported shells each solve it with a native async primitive plus a buffer-preserving repaint call: | Shell | Async notification | Repaint call | | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | `clink.refilterprompt()` | The cycle, with zsh as the reference: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant O as oh-my-posh S->>O: render request (status, duration, pwd, ...) O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt S->>S: cache transient prompt U->>S: presses Enter S->>S: stop updates,
print cached transient prompt S->>U: command executes ``` ## Where this touches the Nushell integration Today's init script (`oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| _omp_get_prompt primary } $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } ``` `_omp_get_prompt` blocks until every segment has finished; a slow git repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is where streaming slots in: request a render, return the first record, and let later records repaint. Everything else maps onto what Nushell already has: the daemon starts once at init and dies with its stdout pipe, the transient prompt uses `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`, and the `pre_execution` hook covers stop-on-accept. ## What Nushell is missing Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle and hands the result to reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From then until the line is submitted, two things are impossible: 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the records, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only processes key events. There is no fd watcher, timer, or job-message delivery that fires while the editor waits for input. 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, and Nushell exposes it as `event: { send: repaint }` in `$env.config.keybindings`, but only a keypress can trigger it, and when it fires, Nushell hands reedline the same stored string. Bind a key to `repaint` and press it mid-edit: the redraw is safe and buffer-preserving, yet the content never changes. Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing: the `ExternalPrinter` is a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle, except the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. ## The ask A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work, in order of preference: **Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred).** A way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a `commandline set-prompt` subcommand. Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a channel polled in reedline's `read_line()` loop that updates the prompt content and repaints. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation. **Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation.** A way for a background job to make Nushell re-run `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` and repaint in place. Also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and runs user code on the engine mid-edit. With Option A, the integration would be roughly: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| job spawn { ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records | each {|record| if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) } else { commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability } } } # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly } ``` Everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today. The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session, and `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a render request per prompt. ## Guarantees from our side - Every record is a complete prompt string; applying only the latest one is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates and the shell never assembles or diffs anything. - The renderer dies on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away; nothing to reap. - On any failure the integration falls back to today's synchronous rendering, and users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

# Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We want to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. ## How it works Users set `"streaming": 100` in their config: a per-segment budget in milliseconds. Segments that exceed it render a placeholder and keep executing; each completion re-renders the full prompt. The same setting enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session, which renders every prompt on request and spares the process spawn each time; a one-shot `oh-my-posh stream` process serves as fallback. Both speak the same protocol: null-byte (`\0`) delimited records on stdout, each a complete, fully escaped prompt string, ready to display as-is. A `\x1e` prefix marks a transient-prompt record the shell caches for Enter. The daemon takes one JSON request per prompt (`{"command":"render","id":42,...}`, via stdin or a named pipe) and prefixes its records with the request id so the shell can drop stale ones; `abort` cancels an in-flight cycle, and losing its stdout pipe kills the daemon, so it dies with the shell. The shell-side integration is thin. It must: 1. **Spawn and read:** run the renderer in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 2. **Repaint on data:** when a record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. 3. **Stop on accept:** on Enter or Ctrl-C, stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output. Requirement 2 is the one Nushell lacks. The four supported shells each solve it with a native async primitive plus a buffer-preserving repaint call: | Shell | Async notification | Repaint call | | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | `clink.refilterprompt()` | The cycle, with zsh as the reference: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant O as oh-my-posh S->>O: render request (status, duration, pwd, ...) O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt S->>S: cache transient prompt U->>S: presses Enter S->>S: stop updates,
print cached transient prompt S->>U: command executes ``` ## Where this touches the Nushell integration Today's init script (`oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| _omp_get_prompt primary } $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } ``` `_omp_get_prompt` blocks until every segment has finished; a slow git repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is where streaming slots in: request a render, return the first record, and let later records repaint. Everything else maps onto what Nushell already has: the daemon starts once at init and dies with its stdout pipe, the transient prompt uses `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`, and the `pre_execution` hook covers stop-on-accept. ## What Nushell is missing Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle and hands the result to reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From then until the line is submitted, two things are impossible: 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the records, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only processes key events. There is no fd watcher, timer, or job-message delivery that fires while the editor waits for input. 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, and Nushell exposes it as `event: { send: repaint }` in `$env.config.keybindings`, but only a keypress can trigger it, and when it fires, Nushell hands reedline the same stored string. Bind a key to `repaint` and press it mid-edit: the redraw is safe and buffer-preserving, yet the content never changes. Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing: the `ExternalPrinter` is a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle, except the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. ## The ask A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work, in order of preference: **Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred).** A way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a `commandline set-prompt` subcommand. Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a channel polled in reedline's `read_line()` loop that updates the prompt content and repaints. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation. **Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation.** A way for a background job to make Nushell re-run `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` and repaint in place. Also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and runs user code on the engine mid-edit. With Option A, the integration would be roughly: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| job spawn { ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records | each {|record| if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) } else { commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability } } } # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly } ``` Everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today. The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session, and `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a render request per prompt. ## Guarantees from our side - Every record is a complete prompt string; applying only the latest one is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates and the shell never assembles or diffs anything. - The renderer dies on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away; nothing to reap. - On any failure the integration falls back to today's synchronous rendering, and users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle, except the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. ## The ask A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work, in order of preference: **Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred).** A way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a `commandline set-prompt` subcommand. Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a channel polled in reedline's `read_line()` loop that updates the prompt content and repaints. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation. **Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation.** A way for a background job to make Nushell re-run `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` and repaint in place. This works too, but costs a closure evaluation per update and runs user code on the engine mid-edit. With Option A, the integration would be roughly: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| job spawn { ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records | each {|record| if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) } else { commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability } } } # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly } ``` Everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today. The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session, and `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a render request per prompt. ## Guarantees from our side - Every record is a complete prompt string; applying only the latest one is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates and the shell never assembles or diffs anything. - The renderer dies on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away; nothing to reap. - On any failure the integration falls back to today's synchronous rendering, and users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

Pass complete — Vale still reports zero errors ([nushell-streaming-analysis.md](C:\Users\jande\AppData\Local\Temp\claude\D--oh-my-posh\85307e10-87a7-49ab-b5ae-047efbfbd20c\scratchpad\nushell-streaming-analysis.md)). The condensed text was already close, so the pass came down to seven targeted fixes:

- **Definite assertions:** "We would like to support it" → "We want to support it."
- **Omit needless words:** the daemon sentence said "per prompt" three times; now "renders every prompt on request and spares the process spawn each time; a one-shot `oh-my-posh stream` process serves as fallback."
- **Exact words:** "Both emit the same protocol" → "Both speak the same protocol" (you emit records, you speak a protocol).
- **Active voice:** "so stale ones can be dropped" → "so the shell can drop stale ones"; and "losing its stdout pipe kills it" → "kills the daemon" (the pronoun could have referred to the request).
- **Untangle a double relative:** "a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls … and which triggers a safe redraw" → split with a semicolon: "reedline polls it during `read_line()` and safely redraws."
- **No fragments:** Option B's "Also works, but…" → "This works too, but…"
- **Keep related words together (Rule 16):** the closing sentence had 20 words between subject and verb ("The single capability that unblocks this, …, is:"). Now the verb comes first: "One capability unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (…): **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**" — the emphatic ask stays in final position.

The document is ready to post.
